name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَجَآءَ إِخْوَةُ يُوسُفَ فَدَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ فَعَرَفَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ (٥٨)
```

```english
[After some years] the brothers of Joseph came and entered his presence. He recognized them, but they did not recognize him. (58)
```

```arabic
وَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُم بِجَهَازِهِمْ قَالَ ٱئْتُونِى بِأَخٍ لَّكُم مِّنْ أَبِيكُمْ ۚ أَلَا تَرَوْنَ أَنِّىٓ أُوفِى ٱلْكَيْلَ وَأَنَا۠ خَيْرُ ٱلْمُنزِلِينَ (٥٩)
```

```english
When he had furnished them with their provision, he said, ‘Bring me a brother that you have through your father. Do you not see that I give the full measure and that I am the best of hosts? (59)
```

```arabic
فَإِن لَّمْ تَأْتُونِى بِهِۦ فَلَا كَيْلَ لَكُمْ عِندِى وَلَا تَقْرَبُونِ (٦٠)
```

```english
But if you do not bring him to me, then there will be no rations for you with me, and don’t [ever] come near me.’ (60)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ سَنُرَٰوِدُ عَنْهُ أَبَاهُ وَإِنَّا لَفَـٰعِلُونَ (٦١)
```

```english
They said, ‘We will solicit him from his father. [That] we will surely do.’ (61)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ لِفِتْيَـٰنِهِ ٱجْعَلُوا۟ بِضَـٰعَتَهُمْ فِى رِحَالِهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَعْرِفُونَهَآ إِذَا ٱنقَلَبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٦٢)
```

```english
He said to his servants, ‘Put their money [back] into their saddlebags. Maybe they will recognize it when they return to their folks, and maybe they will come back [again].’ (62)
```

## (Commentary)

This is another selected chapter from the story of Yusuf (peace be upon him), in which Allah, the Exalted, mentions the arrival of his brothers to him during the years of drought (الجدب) to purchase food for the household of Ya‘qub (peace be upon him). This served as a prelude to Yusuf (peace be upon him) joining his maternal brother to himself—who is the envied one mentioned in the saying of the Exalted, narrating the words of the brothers: **لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلى أَبِينا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ** "Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we are, though we are a [strong] group" (12:8)—and subsequently identifying himself to them and moving the household of Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) from the desert to Egypt.

The reason he did not identify himself to them initially was that he intended to join his maternal brother to himself and to let his paternal brothers see, upon his self-disclosure, the favor Allah had bestowed upon the two of them and the grace He granted them as a result of their godwariness (التقوى) — *the innate caution and protection of the soul against disobeying Allah* — and their patience (الصبر) — *the steadfast endurance in the face of trials* — regarding the harm the brothers had inflicted upon them out of envy (الحسد) and transgression (البغي). Thereafter, he would summon them all. These five verses encompass the story of their entry into Egypt, his proposal that they bring their paternal brother to him if they were to return for the purchase of food and provisions (الميرة), and their acceptance of that proposal.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجاءَ إِخْوَةُ يُوسُفَ فَدَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ فَعَرَفَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُ مُنْكِرُونَ** "And Yusuf’s brothers came and entered unto him. He recognized them, but they did not recognize him" (12:58). There is much omitted in the discourse, the narration of which was left out because no significant purpose pertained to it. The purpose is rather to explain the joining of Yusuf’s maternal brother to him, his sharing in the divine blessing (النعمة) and favor (المن), followed by their recognition of Yusuf and the joining of the household of Ya‘qub to him. Thus, it is a selected portion of his story and what transpired for him after attaining the power (العزة) of Egypt.

Those of his brothers who came to him were the group (العصبة), excluding his maternal brother; for Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) used to find solace in him and would not leave him alone with them after what had happened regarding Yusuf. The evidence for all of this lies in the verses that follow.

Between this entry of theirs upon their brother Yusuf and his appointment over the storehouses of the earth (خزائن الأرض) and his assumption of the power of Egypt after leaving prison, more than seven years had passed. This is because they only came to him during some of the drought years, after the seven years of plenty (المخصبة) had concluded. They had not seen him since they delivered him to the caravan on the day he was taken out of the pit (الجب) while he was a young boy. Years had passed for him in the house of the ‘Aziz, he had remained several years in prison, and he had been in charge of the storehouses for more than seven years. Today, he was in the attire of the ‘Aziz of Egypt; it would not occur to them that he was a Hebrew man rather than a Copt. All of this prevented them from suspecting he was their brother or recognizing him. However, he recognized them through his sagacity (الكياسة) or through the intuition of prophethood (فراسة النبوة), as the Exalted said: **وَجاءَ إِخْوَةُ يُوسُفَ فَدَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ فَعَرَفَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُ مُنْكِرُونَ** "And Yusuf’s brothers came and entered unto him. He recognized them, but they did not recognize him" (12:58).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُمْ بِجَهازِهِمْ قالَ ائْتُونِي بِأَخٍ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَبِيكُمْ أَلا تَرَوْنَ أَنِّي أُوفِي الْكَيْلَ وَأَنَا خَيْرُ الْمُنْزِلِينَ** "When he had furnished them with their provision, he said, ‘Bring me a brother of yours from your father. Do you not see that I give full measure and that I am the best of hosts?" (12:59). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The equipment (الجهاز) is what is prepared of goods and other things, and equipping (التجهيز) is the carrying or sending of that." [End quote]. The meaning is: when he loaded them with the equipment and food he had prepared for them and sold to them, he commanded them to bring to him a brother of theirs from their father, saying: **ائْتُونِي** "Bring me..." etc.

His saying: **أَلا تَرَوْنَ أَنِّي أُوفِي الْكَيْلَ** — meaning, I do not diminish it, nor do I wrong you by relying on my power and might — **وَأَنَا خَيْرُ الْمُنْزِلِينَ** "and that I am the best of hosts" — the most generous to those who stay with me and the best in providing hospitality. This was an inducement for them to return to him a second time and bring their paternal brother. Similarly, his saying in the following verse: **فَإِنْ لَمْ تَأْتُونِي بِهِ فَلا كَيْلَ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي وَلا تَقْرَبُونِ** "But if you do not bring him to me, then you shall have no measure from me, nor shall you come near me" (12:60) is a threat to them so they would not disobey his command. Likewise, their saying in the subsequent verse: **سَنُراوِدُ عَنْهُ أَباهُ وَإِنَّا لَفاعِلُونَ** "They said, ‘We will solicit him from his father, and we will surely do [it]’" (12:61) is an acceptance on their part of that proposal in general and a reassurance for the soul of Yusuf (peace be upon him).

Furthermore, it is understood that his saying (peace be upon him) at the time of their departure: **ائْتُونِي بِأَخٍ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَبِيكُمْ** "Bring me a brother of yours from your father," despite the emphasis, inducement, and threat it contains, is not the kind of speech he would initiate without a preamble or introduction to obscure his identity from them and prevent them from realizing he was Yusuf or suspecting anything that might cause them doubt. This is obvious. The commentators have mentioned many things regarding the story of his negotiations and conversations with them, but there is no evidence for any of it in the speech of the Exalted within the context of the story, nor is there any reliable report in such matters.

The speech of the Exalted is devoid of addressing that; rather, what can be inferred is that he asked them about their affairs, and they—being ten—informed him that they were brothers and that they had another brother who remained with their father. They explained that their father would not part with him, nor would he agree to his departure for travel or otherwise. Thus, the ‘Aziz desired that they bring him to him so he might see him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِنْ لَمْ تَأْتُونِي بِهِ فَلا كَيْلَ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي وَلا تَقْرَبُونِ** "But if you do not bring him to me, then you shall have no measure from me, nor shall you come near me" (12:60). The measure (الكيل) here means the measured thing, which is the food. **وَلا تَقْرَبُونِ** "nor shall you come near me" means do not approach me by entering my land or appearing before me to procure provisions (الامتيار) and purchase food. The meaning of the verse is clear: it is a threat from him to them should they oppose his command, as previously mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا سَنُراوِدُ عَنْهُ أَباهُ وَإِنَّا لَفاعِلُونَ** "They said, ‘We will solicit him from his father, and we will surely do [it]’" (12:61). Solicitation (المراودة), as previously mentioned, is to return to a matter time after time with persistence or artifice. In their saying to Yusuf (peace be upon him), **سَنُراوِدُ عَنْهُ أَباهُ** "We will solicit him from his father," there is evidence that they had related his story to him—that their father was stingy with him, would not agree to part with him, and refused to let him be distant for travel or any absence. Their use of the word **أَباهُ** "his father" rather than "our father" supports this.

Their saying: **وَإِنَّا لَفاعِلُونَ** "and we will surely do [it]" means they would carry out the act of bringing him, or the solicitation to bring him with them and present him to him. The meaning of the verse is clear, and it contains an acceptance of that in general and a reassurance for the soul of Yusuf (peace be upon him), as mentioned before.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقالَ لِفِتْيانِهِ اجْعَلُوا بِضاعَتَهُمْ فِي رِحالِهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَعْرِفُونَها إِذَا انْقَلَبُوا إِلى أَهْلِهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ** "He said to his servants, ‘Put their merchandise in their saddlebags; maybe they will recognize it when they return to their folks, so that they may come back’" (12:62). The servants (الفتيان) is the plural of youth (الفتى), meaning the young pages. Al-Raghib said: "The merchandise (البضاعة) is a substantial portion of wealth set aside for trade." It is said: "He offered merchandise (أبضع بضاعة) and traded it (ابتضعها)." The Exalted said: **هذِهِ بِضاعَتُنا رُدَّتْ إِلَيْنا** "This is our merchandise, returned to us" (12:65), and He said: **بِبِضاعَةٍ مُزْجاةٍ** "with scant merchandise" (12:88). The root of this word is *al-bad‘* (البضع) with a *fathah* on the *ba*, which refers to a piece of meat that is cut (يبضع). He said: "So-and-so is a piece (بضعة) of me," meaning they are like a part of my body due to their closeness. He said: *al-bid‘* (البضع) with a *kasrah* refers to a number detached from ten, used for what is between three and ten; it is also said to be above five and below ten. [End quote]. The saddlebags (الرحال) is the plural of *rahl* (رحل), which refers to containers and belongings. Returning (الانقلاب) means going back.

The meaning of the verse is: Yusuf (peace be upon him) said to his pages: "Put their wealth and merchandise, which they offered as the price for the food they purchased, into their containers, so that they might recognize it when they return to their family—and open the containers—perhaps they will return to us and bring their brother." For this would touch their hearts and make them eager to return and enjoy further honor and kindness.
